perturbation.original_text: 9.1 The Consultant shall perform all services under this Agreement as an "independent contractor" and not as an employee or agent of the Company. The Consultant is not authorized to assume or create any obligation or responsibility, express or implied, on behalf of, or in the name of, the Company or to bind the Company in any manner.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.1 The Consultant shall perform all services under this Agreement as an "independent contractor," however the Company retains the right to dictate the methods and means by which the services are performed. The Consultant is not authorized to assume or create any obligation or responsibility, express or implied, on behalf of, or in the name of, the Company or to bind the Company in any manner.
perturbation.explanation: By adding that the company dictates the 'methods and means' by which the consultant performs services, this contradicts the definition of an independent contractor.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law (M.G.L. c. 149, § 148B) uses a strict three-part test to determine independent contractor status. The modified text contradicts the second part of the test, which requires that 'the service is performed outside the usual course of the business of the employer,' as well as the third part of the test requiring the worker to be 'free from control and direction in connection with the performance of the service.' By retaining the right to dictate the 'methods and means,' the company exerts control, negating independent contractor status.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 148B Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 148B Persons performing service not authorized under this chapter deemed employees; exception Section 148B Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 148B: Persons performing service not authorized under this chapter deemed employees; exception Section 148B. (a) For the purpose of this chapter and chapter 151, an individual performing any service, except as authorized under this chapter, shall be considered to be an employee under those chapters unless:— (1) the individual is free from control and direction in connection with the performance of the service, both under his contract for the performance of service and in fact; and (2) the service is performed outside the usual course of the business of the employer; and, (3) the individual is customarily engaged in an independently established trade, occupation, profession or business of the same nature as that involved in the service performed. (b) The failure to withhold federal or state income taxes or to pay unemployment compensation contributions or workers compensation premiums with respect to an individual's wages shall not be considered in making a determination under this section. (c) An individual's exercise of the option to secure workers' compensation insurance with a carrier as a sole proprietor or partnership pursuant to subsection (4) of section 1 of chapter 152 shall not be considered in making a determination under this section. (d) Whoever fails to properly classify an individual as an employee according to this section and in so doing fails to comply, in any respect, with chapter 149, or section 1, 1A, 1B, 2B, 15 or 19 of chapter 151, or chapter 62B, shall be punished and shall be subject to all of the criminal and civil remedies
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the definition of an independent contractor under Massachusetts law (M.G.L. c. 149, § 148B). The original contract states the consultant is an independent contractor. However, the changed text adds that the company retains the right to dictate the methods and means by which the services are performed. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 148B(a)(1), states that to be considered an independent contractor, 'the individual is free from control and direction in connection with the performance of the service, both under his contract for the performance of service and in fact'. By retaining the right to dictate the 'methods and means,' the company exerts control, which directly contradicts the requirement that the individual is free from control and direction. This creates a legal contradiction because the contract now contains language that is incompatible with the definition of 'independent contractor' under Massachusetts law, making the consultant an employee according to the law.

perturbation.original_text: 6.2 Inventions. (a) All inventions, ideas, creations, discoveries, computer programs, works of authorship, data, developments, technology, designs, innovations and improvements (whether or not patentable and whether or not copyrightable) which are made, conceived, reduced to practice, created, written, designed or developed by the Consultant, solely or jointly with others or under Consultant's direction and whether during normal business hours or otherwise, (i) during the Consultation Period if related to the business of the Company or (ii) during or after the Consultation Period if resulting or directly derived from Proprietary Information (as defined above) (collectively under clauses (i) and (ii), "Inventions"), shall be the sole property of the Company. The Consultant hereby assigns to the Company all Inventions and any and all related patents, copyrights, trademarks, trade names, and other industrial and intellectual property rights and applications therefor, in the United States and elsewhere and appoints any officer of the Company as Consultant's duly authorized attorney to execute, file, prosecute and protect the same before any government agency, court or authority.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.2 Inventions. (a) All inventions, ideas, creations, discoveries, computer programs, works of authorship, data, developments, technology, designs, innovations and improvements (whether or not patentable and whether or not copyrightable) which are made, conceived, reduced to practice, created, written, designed or developed by the Consultant, solely or jointly with others or under Consultant's direction and whether during normal business hours or otherwise, (i) during the Consultation Period if related to the business of the Company or (ii) during or after the Consultation Period if resulting or directly derived from Proprietary Information (as defined above) (collectively under clauses (i) and (ii), "Inventions"), shall be the sole property of the Company. The Consultant hereby assigns to the Company all Inventions, except for moral rights, and any and all related patents, copyrights, trademarks, trade names, and other industrial and intellectual property rights and applications therefor, in the United States and elsewhere and appoints any officer of the Company as Consultant's duly authorized attorney to execute, file, prosecute and protect the same before any government agency, court or authority.
perturbation.explanation: Moral rights are not assignable under the Copyright Act. Stating that the Consultant assigns 'any and all' related rights, including moral rights, is a misstatement of US copyright law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Copyright Act (17 U.S.C. § 106A) grants moral rights to authors of works of visual art, including the rights of attribution and integrity. These rights cannot be transferred or assigned, only waived. The modified text, assigning 'any and all' rights, including moral rights, is a legal impossibility under US Copyright law.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 17 / CHAPTER 1 / § 106A Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 17 USC 106A : Rights of certain authors to attribution and integrity Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 17-COPYRIGHTS CHAPTER 1-SUBJECT MATTER AND SCOPE OF COPYRIGHT Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Effective Date §106A. Rights of certain authors to attribution and integrity (a) Rights of Attribution and Integrity .-Subject to section 107 and independent of the exclusive rights provided in section 106, the author of a work of visual art- (1) shall have the right- (A) to claim authorship of that work, and (B) to prevent the use of his or her name as the author of any work of visual art which he or she did not create; (2) shall have the right to prevent the use of his or her name as the author of the work of visual art in the event of a distortion, mutilation, or other modification of the work which would be prejudicial to his or her honor or reputation; and (3) subject to the limitations set forth in section 113(d), shall have the right- (A) to prevent any intentional distortion, mutilation, or other modification of that work which would be prejudicial to his or her honor or reputation, and any intentional distortion, mutilation, or modification of that work is a violation of that right, and (B) to prevent any destruction of a work of recognized stature, and any intentional or grossly negligent destruction of that work is a violation of that right. (b) Scope and Exercise of Rights .-Only the author of a work of visual art has the rights conferred by subsection (a) in that work, whether or not the author is the copyright owner. The authors of a joint work of visual art are coowners of the rights conferred by subsection (a) in that work. (c) Exceptions .-(1) The modification of a work of visual art which is a result of the passage of time or the inherent nature of the materials is not a distortion, mutilation, or other modification described in subsection (a)(3)(A). (2) The modification of a work of visual art which is the result of conservation, or of the public presentation, including lighting and placement, of the work is not a destruction, distortion, mutilation, or other modification described in subsection (a)(3) unless the modification is caused by gross negligence. (3) The rights described in paragraphs (1) and (2) of subsection (a) shall not apply to any reproduction, depiction, portrayal, or other use of a work in, upon, or in any connection with any item
perturbation.justification: The modified text assigns 'any and all' rights, including moral rights, which is a legal impossibility under US Copyright law. 17 U.S.C. § 106A grants moral rights to authors of works of visual art, including the rights of attribution and integrity. These rights cannot be transferred or assigned, only waived. The scraped snippet from 17 USC 106A explicitly states these rights, noting that the author 'shall have the right to claim authorship of that work' and 'to prevent any intentional distortion, mutilation, or other modification of that work which would be prejudicial to his or her honor or reputation'. The contract's attempt to assign all rights, including these moral rights, directly contradicts the law's protection of these rights for the author.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, the Company and the Consultant desire to establish the terms and conditions under which the Consultant will provide services to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, the Company and the Consultant desire to establish the terms and conditions under which the Consultant will provide essential services to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "services" to "essential services" introduces ambiguity and potential conflict with labor laws regarding employee classification and benefits.
perturbation.law_explanation: The NLRA and subsequent case law distinguish between independent contractors and employees. Describing the consultant's role as providing 'essential services' blurs the line and can suggest an employer-employee relationship, potentially triggering obligations related to collective bargaining, employee benefits, and other protections under the NLRA. The term "essential" implies integration within the company rather than an arms-length contractor relationship.
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes "services" to "essential services," which, according to the Law Explanation, blurs the line between an independent contractor and employee relationship. The Law Explanation states this can potentially trigger obligations related to collective bargaining, employee benefits, and other protections under the NLRA. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable. The term 'essential' implies integration within the company rather than an arms-length contractor relationship. While there are no scraped law snippets, the LLM Law explanation, combined with the Perturbation Explanation clearly indicates a contradiction as 'essential services' misaligns terminology in such a way that it could make the contract unenforceable.
